comment: Prof. Meyers is a really caring professor and he made class interesting and fun! This was my first disability studies course and I learned a lot. I will definitely take him again!

class was easy, i never went to lecture and barely did the homework. when i did attend lecture it was just way too much open discussion w the same two students non stop talking and he should've just stopped letting them ramble.

Overall, this course was interesting with some major flaws along the way. You can tell he is very knowledgeable and experienced in his profession. However, he tries and fails many times to wrap up the open discussion and move through his lecture, mainly because he hasn't taught the course before. Also, his TAs are picky and hard to work with.

I loved this class, Meyers is very kind!! The only thing is that sometimes people would yap too much in our main lecture in a way that wouldn't add value. Despite this, it was his first time teaching this class, and he did a good job. The midterms had a comprehensive study guide, it was evident that he cared and wanted everyone to do well on tests

so...Meyer's class was pretty easy, but I couldn't stand that he would spend 40 minutes of class letting students state their "opinions", which were mostly students reiterating what the person just said. He is an amazing professor, and we had tons of readings. My TA made me question everything after the midterm, but it's a pretty easy class.

By far one of the better professors I have so far at UW. This was his first time teaching an intro class (he typically teaches the upper classes), so there were a few bumps in the road, but he was very transparent and worked hard to ensure we had all the materials we needed. He is very kind and clearly cares deeply about his students.
rockstar professor! 'nuff said.
This was my first LSJ course and it was really good. Meyers is a great lecturer and we covered lots of things. Good discussions in class.

this was his first time teaching LSJ200 so lectures were kind of odd and i hated them in the beginning, but it got better. he's a great teacher & very passionate. lots of reading (like 40-60 pages before each lecture...) study guide given before midterm. pretty good overall, nice guy and powerpoints are available online before class.
Amazing professor attendance isnt graded but you should go to lectures class isnt hard if you pay attention if you study the study guide you will get a good grade

Information in lectures a bit boring, but it's clear the prof tries to make it interesting and is very knowledgable on the information. Graded on a midterm blue book exam (for which we were given some questions before), and two short essays. Readings heavy but can get by without reading them. TAs awesome.
Good professor and interesting class. Lecture can be long, but he can be very funny. The TAs were good too.

One of my all time favorite professors at UW. Professor Meyers is excellent at explaining concepts with examples and engaging with the class, all the while maintaining very fair assignment expectations. His passion for his subject and students shows and makes class a very enjoyable experience, couldn't recommend him more!

Love him! Great professor, really funny and really nice. He really seems to like students, and the class really made me think and see things from a new perspective. Probably one of the best classes I have taken. I will definitely take anything else Stephen teaches.
Great professor, very likeable and understanding. Lectures turn into discussions usually, you can make it just by skimming the readings. The TA was more strict on grading unfortunately.

The topic was new to me, but I found it really interesting. Meyers' lectures are interesting and if you don't do the reading, you will be okay. The only complaint I have is that the TA took forever to get our papers back to us.
Fun professor and really interesting class. He seems to really enjoy teaching and care about the topic. I will definitely take him again.

Nice, well-respected professor. Class content could be a little dry at times and readings could get a little overwhelming, but he definitely gives you a solid foundation to learn/talk about comparative law through a conscientious and intersectional lens. Graded on 3 essays with a cushion of participation/weekly responses. Very easy to approach.

The workload was reasonable and the class was enjoyable because Meyers seems to enjoy the subject. Grading is very dependent on the kind of TA you have when you're in Meyers, but he does set and expectation that extensions are always allowed when reasonably asked for before hand.

I have had him for multiple classes now and the first time I took him the class was so easy, this one was only a little bit harder because my TA was a tough grader. He is a nice and reasonable guy and likes what he is lecturing about.

He represents the worst of JSIS and LSJ politically in terms of his deep investment in international development/imperialism. likable guy and class wasn't too bad until end of quarter, assignments get overwhelming, but very basic humanities class structure and if you speak up he generally listens. cishet abled white man who is trying but not enough
Friendly professor. Lectures are thought-provoking and may turn into (usually friendly) student debates.
Meyers is a likeable guy, truthfully I found him a bit more engaging than what he's actually teaching. Readings for 366 can be a bit dry, but if you write good essays you should be a-OK.

Love love love Professor Meyers! I took his class during COVID so I feel like can't speak to its difficulty, but he was extremely considerate and accommodating. He knew his material well and was easy to understand and talk with.

This was seriously the best class I have ever taken at UW. I had no idea what Social Justice through Philanthropy was going to be, but I learned more about international development, NGOs, and philanthropy in one quarter than my other four years in school.

Good course. Stephen is very smart and very nice. Really interesting topic. Sometimes he can be a little bit disorganized and we always got behind, but it was never a real problem. He also had amazing guest speakers. One of the best professors and classes I have taken. I will definitely take him again.

Meyers was a great professor as long as you show up to the lectures and take good notes your set for the exam, especially since he gives a study guide of what is exactly on the exam. I would say the problem was the TA I had was Yan, she was very unfair on the grading. My recommendation for a good grade is AVIOID YAN, or else your grade will suffer.

Prof. Meyers' disability course was probably the best class I have taken since I have been here. He was really amazing and the topic was super interesting. At first, I wasn't even sure I wanted to take it because I wasn't sure disability was relevant to my international studies major, but I was so wrong: Everyone should take this class.

Steven was honestly one of the best professors I've ever had. He was so kind, and genuinely cared about how your learning was. There were just 2 papers and 2 exams, all of which were very easy. His grading is a little tough, but nothing impossible. He was really passionate about disability and has a lot of field experience.
Good class. Lots of interesting readings and discussion. It really got me thinking. And he is really nice. Would totally take him again.

While the three assigned essays and exams are fairly straightforward (he gives you a clear prompt and tells you almost all of the questions that will be on exams), this prof is honestly a disappointment. Lectures and slides are disorganized and really unclear. Not helpful when you come to him with questions. I would not take a class with him again

He was a very solid professor. He assigned a lot of reading, but if you don't get around to all of it you'll still be fine. He gave thorough feedback on exams and essays to improve, and in the meantime was a generous grader. Very funny, very nice. Very open to helping and discussing outside of class. Very hot.

Meyers is easy if you do the required work. This includes three essays, which are not harshly graded. His midterms are clear, as he gives you a study guide before hand which tells you exactly what will be on the test. If you show up and take notes, the class is a breeze. If you miss, he usually uploads his lecture slides online.

The content of the course on human rights and development was interesting and I was initially excited about the class. Unfortunately course expectations were not clear. His grading was way tougher than any other professor's in LSJ. This was his first quarter teaching at UW so maybe that will change.
